comment.body:
Alma didn't specify which Thursday, but I'm under the impression and would decipher it's this Thursday because she said that there was the chance of selling due to tax loss harvesting (which I believe in) - so before April 15.  

Also, she never said "mean reversion".  She said she noticed that vol selling had begun, and that meant there was POTENTIAL for a grind up.  

Outside of that she said there is a supportive April Open (next Thursday).  However, she has also consistently reiterated that new news can come out and change it all.  The big one we wait for based on my perception of her writings in the EU tariff response, post ECB meeting, which I believe is April 17.

comment.body: From a political perspective, it seems as though Trump is in a box. If he backs off the tariffs, he basically admits he fucked up and he will lose congressional support and become a lame duck. He has to continue with the tariff strategy, at least to a degree that he can point to some realistic gain from his policies. He can’t claim some BS as a win, because then he becomes a lame duck. IMO, they will stick to the strategy despite current turbulence.

He has to continue with the tariff strategy, at least to a degree that he can point to some realistic gain from his policies. He can’t claim some BS as a win, because then he becomes a lame duck. IMO, they will stick to the strategy despite current turbulence.

comment.body: Sure, whether the policy is right or not is TBD. I’m just speculating that the hopium of him changing his mind is not accurately accounting for how Congress works; in particular the house and mid term elections and the fact that Trump can’t run for reelection. comment.body: It's not just replacing cheap labour with automation. There's also the fact that a lot of industrial manufacturing produces a shit ton of pollution, and there are hardly any environmental rules/regulations in third world countries like China (formerly), India, Mexico, etc.  You're not gonna be able to bring back as much manufacturing as you might think, especially with NIMBYism

comment.body: First, they’re not selling debt back to the US gov’t.  They’re selling to other entities.  Second, you should think of it in terms of supply and demand.  If they start selling, that’s more supply that needs to be absorbed and prices could go down (yields and this interest rates go up).  Plus if they were net buyers in the past and they are no longer buying, then demand goes down so price is getting hit on both sides of the supply/demand curves.
comment.raw_body: First, they’re not selling debt back to the US gov’t.  They’re selling to other entities.  Second, you should think of it in terms of supply and demand.  If they start selling, that’s more supply that needs to be absorbed and prices could go down (yields and this interest rates go up).  Plus if they were net buyers in the past and they are no longer buying, then demand goes down so price is getting hit on both sides of the supply/demand curves.

comment.body: Investors increasingly view the tariffs as ideological rather than strategic, potentially damaging the US's reputation for stability and prompting de-risking in dollar-based assets (e.g., gold, Treasuries). Capital flows into the US, long incentivized and rewarded, may shift as foreign investors and corporates reassess exposure amid uncertainty. Private markets, including private equity and credit, may face pressure to adjust valuations as public markets decline and recession risks rise.
